data.title: Trump's Impact On The Labor Market
data.description:
By Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist

The Trump administration’s bold policy initiatives to increase government efficiency and tighten immigration will likely have macroeconomic consequences for the labor market, potentially weighing on labor demand and supply.

However, the combined effect will likely also be very nuanced, suggesting the data over the coming months will be difficult to parse and understand, making policymaking even more challenging for the Fed.

The Trump administration has unleashed bold policy initiatives on government efficiency and immigration that are likely to impact the labor market, though in a very nuanced way.

Plans for a wide-scale reduction in government workforce would likely have limited direct impact, especially as the federal workforce only makes up 2% of total nonfarm payrolls. Moreover, while government jobs have increasingly driven payroll gains in recent years - making up 23% of jobs created since August 2022 - only 3% is attributable to the federal government.

Outsourced government work has grown significantly, however, so a reduction in contract spending could pose bigger challenges. Such a reduction would likely dampen labor demand for impacted downstream industries, including professional services, healthcare, and transportation.

Meanwhile, the administration’s goal to implement stricter immigration measures is unlikely to meaningfully impact the flow of labor, especially as immigration flows had already begun normalizing in late-2024.

However, with foreign-born labor making up over half of labor force growth since 2023, increased immigration crackdowns could significantly affect the stock of labor. Tighter immigration policy could reduce labor supply in industries most reliant on immigrant labor: construction, accommodation and food services.

The combined effect of these policies will likely make the labor data over the coming months difficult to parse and understand. Already having to navigate tariff uncertainties, this only makes policymaking even more challenging for the Fed.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Investing For Security In Unstable Times: Energy And Supply Chains
data.description:
By James MacGregor, CFA | Cem Inal | Luke Pryor

Efforts to secure supply chains and energy sources are creating powerful and enduring themes for equity investors - even in these turbulent times.

Investors are digesting a barrage of US policy decisions and global technology news that has fueled market volatility. Despite the uncertainties, we think some broad trends will ultimately transcend the market noise and offer attractive opportunities for equity investors with a long-term mindset.

Two major forces have unsettled markets in early 2025. Chinese company DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) unveiled a generative artificial intelligence (AI) model that may have been developed at a fraction of the cost of existing models, raising questions about future AI-related spending. Meanwhile, President Trump's plans to impose significant tariffs on major trading partners has triggered extraordinary instability, with the Federal Reserve's Economic Policy Uncertainty Index for trade jumping to a near record high (Display).

Uncertainty over Economic and Trade Policy Has Spiked

Economic Policy Uncertainty Index: Categorical Index - Trade Policy

When dramatic news upends markets, investors may lose confidence in strategic investments. Yet, moments like these also create fertile ground for active investors to stay focused on the businesses best positioned to benefit from durable trends.

AI and Energy Security: Myths and Facts

The energy transition is a good example. DeepSeek's breakthrough has raised questions about the future trajectory of investment in the transition to a more robust domestic power system and US energy security. What's the connection? Perhaps, some argue, lower development costs of energy-intensive AI technology might reduce the investment needed to improve and upgrade US energy infrastructure below previous forecasts.

We think that conclusion misses the mark because most of the projected rise in energy and power infrastructure investment has nothing to do with AI. In fact, energy investment is primarily being driven by the need to modernize an aging US power grid and to ensure that generation capacity can support more US manufacturing and consumer demand. And rising demand for US power requires secure energy sources.

US power demand is projected to grow by about 2.5% a year through 2030, according to Goldman Sachs's estimates (Display), with AI accounting for about a fifth of total growth. As we see it, this implies that even if AI growth falls short of expectations, power demand will still probably grow by at least 2% a year. So continued investment in upgrading US energy infrastructure and securing supply is still vital. If AI spending exceeds expectations, it will make a strong case for energy security even more compelling, in our view.

US Power Demand Is Poised for Growth Regardless of AI Trajectory

What types of businesses could benefit? We believe companies that manufacture equipment or infrastructure for electrification should continue to enjoy solid growth as the quest for energy security plays out. Similarly, equipment makers for industries like natural gas and nuclear energy, which are essential for meeting higher demand, should also see steady gains.

Tariffs and Trade Wars Buoy Supply Chain Investment

President Trump has been pressing ahead with plans to impose tariffs on Canada, Mexico and China, as well as a wider set of trading partners. The situation is fluid, but the brewing trade war has compounded concerns about the broader industrial economy and levels of capital investment needed to restore US infrastructure.

While the anxiety is understandable, we think the trade war will actually add another catalyst for US companies to secure supply chains, which in turn, will likely bolster infrastructure investment. For many companies, this is already happening. Since the COVID-19 pandemic disrupted global supply chains, companies have been reconfiguring supply chains to reduce the risk of painful production bottlenecks. Concerns about US-China trade tensions have already led many US companies that depended on Chinese suppliers to find alternate sources of supply elsewhere.

Massive spending to secure supply chains is taking place in areas tied to US national security and economic priorities, such as in the semiconductor industry. If anything, the latest round of tariffs has incentivized companies to invest even more to reduce supply chain risk.

Decaying US infrastructure is an obstacle to this process. For years, chronic underinvestment in US ports, airports, roads and power has been a festering problem, which must be addressed in any strategic policy agenda aimed at revitalizing American manufacturing and securing supply chains. Yet in January, Trump sought to eliminate or cut certain components of the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act of 2021. In our view, infrastructure investment remains an area of rare bipartisan agreement. So after the dust settles, we expect spending on key initiatives to resume, given the urgency.

Despite near-term policy concerns, recent earning trends indicate that some industries are poised to benefit from the push to secure supply chains. Semiconductor equipment is showing signs of recovery even as sales to China slow, based on revenue from Lam Research, one of the industry's leaders (Display). Logistics in general - and trucking in particular - are stabilizing following a steep decline from pandemic-driven peaks. Spending on cybersecurity continues to power ahead through the turbulence we have seen in the other parts of the economy.

Fortifying Supply Chains: Which Industries May Benefit?

For equity investors, security-oriented themes offer a path to capturing attractive sources of long-term returns in an uncertain environment.

Start by identifying industries that are well positioned to capitalize on US efforts to achieve energy independence and supply chain security. Then, use fundamental research to find companies that are exposed to the themes with shares that trade at a discount because of the uncertainty.

To be sure, investors must be attuned to the rapidly changing policy risks. But with a thematic approach to energy and supply chain security, and an emphasis on quality businesses with competitive advantages, we think investors can create an equity portfolio that is positioned to surmount market volatility and to capture resilient sources of long-term return potential.

References to specific securities discussed are for illustrative purposes only and are not to be considered recommendations by AllianceBernstein L.P.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Agnico Eagle Mines Limited: A Long-Term Hold For A Class Act
data.description:
Introduction

Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (NYSE:AEM) is the mining operation I would own if I could only own one mine.

Agnico-Eagle is large successful gold mining company and it has been analyzed so often as to now have a high profile in the world of precious metals producers. I have traded this stock since the 1980s and it has been good to me. However on some occasions I have looked to make a purchase but low-balled the bid to hopefully get a bargain and have failed to acquire any shares. The reason that I own shares in Agnico-Eagle is that they came into my hands by the merger and acquisitions route. On the 12th of February, 2020 I was a shareholder in Detour Gold Corp. (DRGDF) when they were acquired by Kirkland Lake Gold Limited (KL) (TSX:KL) and as I liked both companies I held onto the stock. On the 8th February 2022, along came Agnico-Eagle and merged with Kirkland Lake Gold Ltd. Although I thought Agnico’s shares were expensive at the time at around $52.00 I decided to stick with them given the quality and the size of the company who were well positioned to do well in the upcoming Bull Market in the precious metals space. Agnico-Eagle has since flirted with the $100.00 price tag and so I need to decide if it is a Buy, a Sell or a Hold and just how would I implement such a decision.

Agnico Eagle Mines Limited: Brief Description

Agnico Eagle Mines is a $49.25B gold mining company founded in 1957 and has since grown into one of three largest gold producers in the world alongside Newmont Corporation (NEM) and Barrick Gold Corp (GOLD) Traditionally gold producers have rarely paid out a dividend whereas Agnico has consistently paid out a cash dividend since 1983 which is no mean feat especially when times where very hard for this industry. The following picture is an ariel view of one of Agnico’s mines looking a tad cold and covered in snow.

They have their headquarters in Toronto, Canada from where they manage operations in Canada, Australia, Mexico, and Finland, from which their income is derived.

Financials

We will commence with a quick look at the highlights of its fourth quarter and full year 2024 results. I tend to look at those metrics that are important to me and are of value to my decision-making process but I suggest that you read them thoroughly and satisfy yourself that you have a good understanding of Agnico’s ability to perform before you hit the acquisition trail.

One would expect that the spokesperson for the company would have something positive to say about the company and Ammar Al-Joundi, the President and Chief Executive Officer, stated the following:

"I'm pleased to report on another year of record operational and financial performance, achieving our production and cost guidance. This success, along with capital discipline, has enabled us to reduce net debt by $1.3 billion since the beginning of the year and return close to $1.0 billion dollars to our shareholders,"

This is a positive introduction and it suggests that investors have placed their funds with Agnico are safe hands.

One of the metrics that I check is the all-in sustaining costs ("AISC") achieved which is $1,230/Oz on an annual basis, compared with $1,179/Oz for the year ending 2023. But it should be noted that for this quarter they were $1,316/Oz so this is significant increase and something we need to be cognizant of.

This increase is due to higher cash costs per ounce along with sustaining capital expenditures mainly due to their operations at Canadian Malartic, Goldex and Kittila mine sites.

Other standouts that interest me include:

The Payable gold production was 847,401ounces for the quarter and 3,485,336 ounces for 2024 which is as per the company’s previous guidance. This is important to investors that production comes in close to their guidance figures as failure to do so can lead to a loss in investor confidence, a withdrawal of investor funds and a drop in the stock price. For the year of 2024 operating activities generated cash of $3,961 million along with a free cash flow of $2,143 million, which I think is a good result. There is also an increase in gold reserves of 0.9% to 54.3 million ounces which doesn’t sound like a lot but for a large operator it is important to keep the pipeline full thus enabling mining for years to come.

Now we come to debt reduction and maybe it’s because I’m ‘old school’ that this is something that I like to see as during the fourth quarter a sum of $325 million was repaid.

More good news is that for investors looking for an income from their holdings is that Agnico announced a quarterly dividend of $0.40 per share. Another plus is that they bought back 248,700 common shares at an average share price of $80.39, which not only reduces the stock available for purchasers but also looks to be a good move given that the current share price is flirting with the $100.00 share price.

Stable and continuous production is a vital element when viewing any company’s future prospects and this update includes a new three-year estimate which is a tad lower previously stated. This is due largely to the deferral of processing low margin ore. This is not something that I will lose sleep over as the expectation is to produce 3.3 to 3.5 million ounces annually from 2025 to 2027.

I do urge you to read these reports thoroughly and gain a good appreciation of Agnico’s value as I tend to look at those metrics that have an impact on my investment criteria, which are different from yours as we all have differing financial goals, aversion to risk etc.

A quirk of mine is to observe the after-hour trading after the issuance of reports such as this one and to my surprise the stock price fell from $100.81 to touch $97.00. A knee jerk reaction may be due to the revenue being reported a tad lower than expectations. Anyway by 8pm the price had recovered to trade at $100.35. The next week or so will give us an indication of just how the investment community feels about these figures. And of course the movement in the price of gold will feed into all the producers involved in the precious metals space.

For a forward projection, a quick look at Seeking Alpha's Quant section has a PE ('FWD') of 24.06 and an EPS ('FWD') of 4.14, with a rating of “Hold”

Agnico is ranked in its Industry at 16 out of 41 with a score of 3.30 which places Agnico below a number of its peers including Newmont Corporation (NEM) and Barrick Gold Corporation (GOLD)

The liquidity is good with an average volume of 2,682,708 shares traded per day enabling short-term traders to enter and exit this stock with relative ease.

Agnico Eagle is traded on both the NYSE under the ticker symbol of AEM and in Canada under the symbol of AEM.TO.

Agnico Eagle Mines Limited: One Year Chart

Agnico has more than doubled from a low of $44.00 in February 2024 to $100.00 of late.

The RSI and the MACD have peaked in the overbought zone suggesting that breather is due.

A number of investors are now sitting on handsome profits and given that this stock is technically in the overbought zone the temptation to bank those profits will be difficult to resist and so some selling may ensue over the next week or so.

Stock Comparison Chart

Agnico is one the biggest gold miners in the world alongside Newmont Corporation (NEM) and Barrick Gold Corp (GOLD) and the following chart depicts how all three faired over a one-year period.

As we can see Agnico more than doubled over that time frame, which is a considerable achievement for a large market cap company. There are of course gold mining enterprises with a smaller market capitalization that have performed even better than Agnico such as Kinross Gold Corp (KGC), which has a market capitalization of around $14.8B and it gained around 131% over the same period.

A Quick Look At The Chart Of Gold Prices

Note the formation of a Triple Top of $2800/Oz, and that gold has rallied above it to a new all-time high

The RSI is high suggesting that gold is now overbought and may correct in the near-term. Gold’s rally started in March 2024 and peaked in November 2024. Having taken a short breather gold has once again rallied to higher ground. The MACD has formed a positive crossover but it is close to the top of its range suggesting a pullback may be imminent

The Risks that are worthy of some consideration

Agnico's fortunes are largely dependent upon gold's ability to continue to rally to higher ground. Gold has a number of competitors for investors funds including the stock market in general, the US dollar, the crypto market, real estate, etc. which all have various merits and attract large amounts of capital.

Other risks would include but are not limited to changes brought about by the governing bodies in each of their mining jurisdictions. A new government can impose new restrictions which could result in a larger share of their profits being retained by the host nation.

Mining is also fraught with various difficulties and I would like to use the following example to demonstrate an event that can occur at any time. On 11th October 2011 Agnico announced that they had to write off a project known as the Goldex Mine. Below is the chart we used in this article accompanied with the following narrative:

“News that the Goldex Mine is to be written off as hammered Agnico-Eagle Mines Limited (NYSE:AEM) today. The loss is estimated to be around $1.00 per share, however, investors moved fast to dump the stock, taking it down $10.58 or 18.55% on volume of 13 million shares traded in today's session on the NYSE. If ever we needed a reminder of just how difficult a mining operation can be, this is it.”

This sort of disaster doesn’t happen very often but when it does it can severely dent your portfolio as these events do take some time from which to recover.

So as always go gently with your investments and ensure that you can sleep at night even when some of your stocks head south.

Conclusion

Agnico Eagle Mines Limited has more than doubled over the last twelve months proving that it is a class act in the precious metals space.

The technical analysis suggests that Agnico is currently overbought and a near term correction could be imminent. You could protect your gains by selling some of your stock and banking the profits or you could buy a few Put Options which would be the beneficiary of a sudden pullback in Agnico’s share price.

At the moment I am content to hold this position as I am of the opinion that the Bull Market in precious metals has a long way to go and this stock will be one the market leaders.

On two occasions have given this stock a ‘Strong Buy’ rating and I have no reason to down grade it so it remains a ‘Strong Buy’ for me.

Your comments are very much appreciated whether you agree with me or not, so please fire them in, and I will do my best to address each and every one of them.

Go gently.

data.title: Fiserv: Attractive Earnings Growth Equity Story
data.description:
Summary

I am positive about Fiserv (NYSE:FI). My thesis is that FI continues to grow top line while expanding margin, leading to mid-to-high-teens earnings growth. The main growth driver will be its Clover platform and the broader enterprise solutions. As FI expands its merchant acquiring network, deepens its integration with payroll services, and monetizes VAS, I see a clear path for continued earnings growth.

Company overview

FI is one of the many players in the payments and fintech industry. For payments, FI offers a range of services including merchant acquiring, banking, core processing, and issuing solutions. Clover is FI’s core platform, which is a cloud-based point-of-sale [POS] system for small and medium-sized businesses [SMBs]. As for the fintech side of things, FI solutions help clients modernize operations, such as digital banking and core processing.

Earnings results update

FI reported outstanding 4Q24 results, which, once again, demonstrated FI’s ability to deliver consistent earnings growth. While adj revenue grew 7% y/y to $4.90 billion, which is slightly below consensus expectations of $4.95 billion, FI went on to beat consensus adj EPS estimates of $2.48 with its reported adj EPS of $2.51. Other key takeaways from the quarter that point to solid growth momentum include:

  • Organic revenue grew 13% y/y, led by 23% growth in merchant solutions and 4% in financial solutions
  • Adj EBIT margin expanded 180 bps y/y to 42.9%
  • Clover revenue grew 29% y/y, maintaining its high-20s growth trajectory
  • SMBs revenue accelerated back to double digits (12% y/y growth) after dipping to 9% in 3Q24
  • Enterprise merchant revenue grew 15% y/y reportedly and 31% on an organic growth basis

While some investors may focus on the revenue miss, I think the overall takeaway was that FI’s equity story remains as a strong EPS grower, wherein it was able to grow revenue and expand EBIT margins, and that Clover continues to be a growth driver (more below).

Clover’s Growth Remains a Core Strength

One of the concerns about FI’s equity story was that Clover—one of the main growth drivers—will slow down due to the tough macro environment that is hurting SMBs (FY22 and FY23 saw 25% y/y growth). I believe FI’s FY24 performance has put this concern to rest as revenue growth accelerated to 29% y/y, with 4Q24 growing by 29% y/y, setting up a good baseline to think about FY25’s growth. Even though gross payment volume [GPV] growth slowed slightly to 14% y/y from 15% in 3Q24 (and 17% y/y in 4Q23), I thought it shone a very positive light on FI’s pricing power and the value of its value-added services [VAS]. The latter (regarding VAS adoption) is one of the more underappreciated aspects of Clover’s growth. FI has done a really great job in driving adoption (seen via SaaS penetration, which increased to 22% from 21% in 3Q24 and 19% in 4Q23), which, I think, is driven by its constant rolling out of new product offerings, including Clover Kiosk, aimed at quick-service restaurants and retail; Kitchen Display System, designed to improve restaurant order flow; and Clover Flex Pocket, a mobile handheld POS solution. In my view, this extends Clover’s growth runway as it is able to monetize the ecosystem beyond basic payment processing.

As a note, I actually think the slower volume growth makes sense given that the macro backdrop is not the best. Which means, in a normalized macro environment, Clover’s growth could go past 30% y/y. Management guidance for Clover revenue in FY25 supports my view too, with revenue expected to reach $3.5 billion in 2025, implying high-20s% growth. While it is hard to quantify, FI's recent expansion into Brazil, Mexico, and Australia should also further support growth momentum.​

Mentioned in the 4Q24 earnings call: “Drilling down by segment, for Merchant Solutions, we see organic revenue growth of 12% to 15% in 2025, driven mostly by strong growth in Clover as we reach our $3.5 billion revenue target, including an increase in VAS penetration to the 25% outlook”

Well positioned to continue growing Merchant Business

FI’s broader merchant solutions performance is noteworthy as well, as it delivered another strong quarter, with revenue growth of 11% y/y (23% y/y on an organic basis). The growth outlook remains very bright, in my opinion, as FI continues to expand its distribution capacity by adding 100 new financial institution [FIS] partners in 2024 and expanding its acquiring network to ~1,000 banks. I see this as a massive competitive advantage, as FI can effectively tap into these FIS partners' pool of clients and sales representatives to distribute its products. This contrasts with players like Square and Toast that need to acquire customers directly.

FI's recent partnership with ADP (integrating payroll services directly into the Clover ecosystem) can also be seen as a growth catalyst. The nature of SMBs is that they lack manpower and expertise to better handle the back-end stuff (like payroll, time tracking, and benefits management, etc.). They tend to rely on disparate systems to “get the business going” since their primary focus is to drive sales and keep the business afloat. The problem is that these separate systems don't always integrate well with their POS solutions, and this is a competitive disadvantage vs. peers that have such integration in place (peers can do better customer analysis to understand consumer preferences, roll out loyalty programs, etc.).

Hence, by embedding ADP’s payroll services directly into Clover, FI is creating a seamless, all-in-one platform where business owners can manage both payments and the broader human capital management offerings (like payroll, tax filing, etc.) from a single interface. What makes this even more compelling is that ADP will be reselling Clover through its own distribution channels, which could significantly expand FI reach in the SMB space.

Valuation

I think there is still upside to FI’s stock price. Using management's FY25 11% growth guidance and extrapolating that forward, which is definitely possible given the growth catalysts I mentioned above, it should generate ~$26 billion in revenue in FY27. FI has also proven its ability to expand margin consistently through the years, which I expect to continue due to operating leverage and the growing adoption of higher-margin VAS products. The beauty of having a business that can grow earnings at a solid pace is that we don’t need multiples to go up from here to make attractive returns. I simply assumed FI to remain trading at 22x forward PE, and there is a 29% upside. Even if you assume that FI sees a mean reversion in multiples to 20x (which I don’t see a reason it should), the upside is still attractive at 17%.

Investment Risk

Although I like FI competitive advantages, that is not to say that the payment solution industry is not competitive. Square and Toast are very strong competitors that have a track record of growing rapidly. If they have managed to eventually get into a partnership with banks, it could pressure Clover’s growth. Macroeconomic risks like higher interest rates could also put further pressure on underlying consumer spending power, which hurts SMBs, and thereby reduce the pool of prospective clients that FI can capture in the near term.

Conclusion

My positive view on FI is because I like the earnings growth story, supported by strong growth drivers. Clover continues to perform strongly and should continue to do so given the growing international footprint and increasing VAS penetration. Additionally, merchant solutions continue to scale, benefiting from FI partnerships and the ADP payroll integration. Importantly, even if multiples stay at the current level, the upside is attractive.

data.title: Janus Henderson Opportunistic Alpha Managed Account Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

Equity markets extended gains in the fourth quarter, building on strong performance through the first nine months of 2024. The U.S. economy remained resilient, as third-quarter 2024 gross domestic product (GDP) growth came in at 2.8% and the unemployment rate held steady around 4.1%. Equities reacted favorably to the decisive Republican victory in the November election given expectations for expansionary fiscal policies and deregulation. After starting the rate-cutting cycle with a 50-basis-point (bp) cut in September and two additional 25 bp rate cuts in November and December, the Federal Reserve (Fed) struck a decidedly more hawkish tone at its December meeting, with the revised Fed dot plot implying just 50 bps of rate cuts in 2025. Market gains reflected a narrow advance: only four of the 11 S&P 500 (SP500, SPX) sectors posted gains, the equal-weighted S&P 500 lagged its market cap counterpart, and the Magnificent Seven significantly outperformed the broader market. Further, large cap led small cap and growth stocks outperformed value stocks.

Portfolio review

Semiconductor company Marvell Technology (MRVL) was a top contributor to relative performance. The company's third-quarter results exceeded expectations, driven by strong demand for its AI chips. Its expanded partnership with Amazon Web Services (AWS), driven by the growth of Amazon’s (AMZN) next generation Trainium AI chip, strengthened Marvell’s market position in AI semiconductors.

Madrigal Pharmaceuticals (MDGL) was also among the top contributors. The company is focused on developing therapies for metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH), or fatty liver disease, which affects over 10 million adults in the U.S. and is a leading cause of liver failure. Madrigal reported third-quarter revenues for MASH treatment Rezdiffra that doubled consensus expectations. The company also achieved its 80% commercial coverage goal earlier than expected. Finally, shares responded positively after Novo Nordisk (NVO) reported modest efficacy for its GLP-1 drug semaglutide for MASH, possibly bringing the pharma giant’s marketing prowess to build the MASH market while still leaving room for Rezdiffra to augment activity.

Surgery Partners (SGRY), which owns and operates a network of surgical facilities and ancillary services in the U.S., was among the top detractors from relative performance. The stock declined following disappointing third-quarter earnings, with no sale update despite private equity’s 40% ownership stake and weak cash flow due to transaction costs and increased mergers and acquisitions (M&A) spending.

Despite these short-term challenges, the investment thesis remains compelling, driven by the ongoing shift from high-cost hospitals to lower-cost surgery centers. With historically low valuations and potential regulatory tailwinds, we believe the growth outlook remains attractive.

Casino operator Caesars Entertainment (CZR) was also among the top detractors. The company lowered guidance for regional gaming performance in 2025, which pressured the stock. In addition, its leveraged balanced sheet made it particularly vulnerable to rising interest rates following the election.

Despite these headwinds, Caesars maintains strong fundamentals in its core Las Vegas operations and has shown positive progress in its digital gaming segment.

Manager outlook

The U.S. economy continues to show resilience, with sturdy GDP growth projected into next year and a strong labor market supporting consumer spending. Earlier concerns about a potential hard landing have largely dissipated, with discussions now centered on the likelihood of either a soft landing or no-landing scenario.

The Fed’s shift toward a more neutral monetary policy, combined with economic strength, creates a positive environment for risk assets and broader market opportunities. While the Magnificent Seven companies are expected to maintain higher earnings growth rates than the other 493 S&P 500 companies, this gap has been narrowing. As earnings growth expands, performance leadership should extend beyond tech giants. This diversification could benefit our strategy due to the portfolio’s varied exposures across segments, sizes, and styles.

Further, the election results and the Trump administration's pro-growth policies have been viewed as market-friendly. We anticipate that a deregulatory environment could be conducive to increased M&A activity and investment, particularly benefitting small- and mid-cap stocks.

In technology, secular trends around AI remain firmly intact. Capital investment in AI infrastructure has reached unprecedented levels. Rising consumer adoption and demand for AI products and services could benefit companies in several sectors, from semiconductor chipmakers and data centers to companies powering data centers and those engineering AI applications.

Beyond tech, compelling opportunities exist in overlooked areas, including financials. We believe the sector is well positioned following the election as longer-term rates have risen. The steeper yield curve, reflecting the wider gap between short-term borrowing rates and long-term lending rates, leads to higher net interest margins for banks. At the same time, the healthy economy and less activist regulation should particularly benefit the financial sector.

While the market remains constructive for risk assets, market concentration remains high, with the top 10 stocks comprising about 39% of the S&P 500. Market breadth has narrowed considerably, with leadership concentrated among the largest technology companies. However, this market bifurcation has created areas of attractive valuations. We believe portfolio diversification and a focus on undervalued opportunities could provide advantages in this environment.

Overall, we see a favorable backdrop for U.S. equities supported by positive AI secular growth, broadening earnings growth, and a more accommodative monetary policy. We believe the portfolio is well positioned for an environment where the market broadens and the equal- weighted S&P Index performs well against its cap-weighted counterpart.

Portfolio management

Nick Schommer, CFA

Definitions

Basis point (BP) equals 1/100 of a percentage point. 1 bp = 0.01%, 100 bps = 1%. Diversification neither assures a profit nor eliminates the risk of experiencing investment losses.

Monetary policy refers to the policies of a central bank, aimed at influencing the level of inflation and growth in an economy. It includes controlling interest rates and the supply of money. A yield curve plots the yields (interest rate) of bonds with equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. Typically bonds with longer maturities have higher yields.

The S&P 500® Equal Weight Index (EWI) is the equal-weight version of the widely used S&P 500. The index includes the same constituents as the capitalization-weighted S&P 500, but each company in the S&P 500 EWI is allocated a fixed weight or 0.2% of the index total at each quarterly rebalance.

To receive a complete list and description of composites and/or a presentation that complies with the requirements of the GIPS® standards, please contact Janus Henderson at 800.668.0434. The opinions are as of 12/31/24, are subject to change and may not reflect the views of others in the organization. Janus Henderson may have a business relationship with certain entities discussed. The comments should not be construed as a recommendation of individual holdings or market sectors, but as an illustration of broader themes.

Opportunistic Alpha Managed Account Composite, benchmarked to the S&P 500 Index, includes portfolios that seek to invest in under-valued companies with improving return on invested capital and an asymmetrical risk/reward profile. The portfolios may have exposure to foreign securities through ADRs. A typical portfolio invests in 35 to 55 equity securities. Prior to June 1, 2007 returns for the composite are for the Institutional Opportunistic Alpha Composite, which consisted of separately managed institutional accounts as well as sub-advised pooled funds. The composite was created in June 2007.

Information relating to portfolio holdings is based on the representative account in the composite, which reflects the typical portfolio management style of the investment strategy. Other accounts in the strategy may vary due to asset size, client guidelines and other factors.

Portfolio holdings are as of the date indicated, and are subject to change. This material should not be construed as recommendation to buy or sell any security. Holdings are subject to change without notice.

For equity portfolios, relative contribution compares the performance of a security in the portfolio to the benchmark’s total return, factoring in the difference in weight of that security in the benchmark. Returns are calculated using daily returns and previous day ending weights rolled up by ticker, gross of advisory fees, may exclude certain derivatives and does not represent actual performance.

There is no assurance the stated objective(s) will be met.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal and fluctuation of value.

Discussion is based on performance gross of fees and expenses.

Smaller capitalization securities may be less stable and more susceptible to adverse developments, and may be more volatile and less liquid than larger capitalization securities.

Growth stocks are subject to increased risk of loss and price volatility and may not realize their perceived growth potential.

Value stocks can continue to be undervalued by the market for long periods of time and may not appreciate to the extent expected.

Funds classified as “nondiversified” can take larger positions in a smaller number of issuers than “diversified” funds, which could lead to greater volatility.

Investing in overlooked or underappreciated companies presents special risks. Stocks can continue to be undervalued by the market for long periods of time and may not appreciate to the extent expected.

Actively managed portfolios may fail to produce the intended results. No investment strategy can ensure a profit or eliminate the risk of loss.

Actively managed investment portfolios are subject to the risk that the investment strategies and research process employed may fail to produce the intended results. Accordingly, a portfolio may underperform its benchmark index or other investment products with similar investment objectives.

S&P 500® Index reflects U.S. large-cap equity performance and represents broad U.S. equity market performance.

Index returns are provided to represent the investment environment existing during the periods shown. The index is fully invested, including the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Index returns do not include any transaction costs, management fees or other costs, and are gross of non-reclaimable withholding taxes, if any and unless otherwise noted.

Janus Henderson Investors claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®). For the purpose of claiming GIPS compliance, Janus Henderson Investors defines its GIPS Firm as the following entities within Janus Henderson Group plc that directly manage assets: Janus Henderson Investors UK Limited, Janus Henderson Investors (Singapore) Limited (excluding private equity assets), Janus Henderson Fund Management UK Limited, Janus Henderson Investors US LLC and Janus Henderson Investors (Australia) Institutional Funds Management Limited. The GIPS firm was formed on January 1, 2018 as a result of the merger of the predecessor GIPS firms Janus Capital Management LLC and Henderson Global Investors, which previously claimed compliance since January 1, 1994 and January 1, 2009, respectively.

Janus Henderson provides investment advisory services in the U.S. through Janus Henderson Investors US LLC, together with its participating affiliates. Janus Henderson is a trademark of Janus Henderson Group plc or one of its subsidiaries. © Janus Henderson Group plc.

GIPS® is a registered trademark of CFA Institute. CFA Institute does not endorse or promote this organization, nor does it warrant the accuracy or quality of the content contained herein.

data.title: Dividend Champion, Contender, And Challenger Highlights: Week Of February 16
data.description:
Introduction

The Dividend Champions list is a monthly compilation of companies which have consistently increased their annual dividend payouts. However, since this list is only produced once per month, the data in it can quickly get out of date. Furthermore, with around 700 companies on the list, the sheer amount of data can quickly become overwhelming. In this weekly series, I highlight recent and upcoming dividend related activity for dividend stocks on the Dividend Champions list.

In the data presented below, Yield is forward annualized and Years reflects the up-to-date streak, including dividends declared since the last edition of the Dividend Champions list.

data.title: Retail Sales Tumble 0.9% In January, Worse Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Census Bureau's Advance Retail Sales Report for January showed a sharper-than-expected decline, with headline sales falling 0.9% - the largest monthly drop since March 2023 and the first decline in five months. Meanwhile, December's figures were revised upward to a 0.7% gain. The latest data came in weaker than the anticipated 0.2% drop in consumer spending.

For an inflation-adjusted perspective on retail sales, take a look at our Real Retail Sales commentary.

Here is the introduction from Friday's report:

Advance estimates of U.S. retail and food services sales for January 2025, adjusted for seasonal variation and holiday and trading-day differences, but not for price changes, were $723.9 billion, down 0.9 percent (±0.5 percent) from the previous month, and up 4.2 percent (±0.5 percent) from January 2024. Total sales for the November 2024 through January 2025 period were up 4.2 percent (±0.5 percent) from the same period a year ago. The November 2024 to December 2024 percent change was revised from up 0.4 percent (±0.5 percent)* to up 0.7 percent (±0.3 percent).

Retail trade sales were down 1.2 percent (±0.5 percent) from December 2024, and up 4.0 percent (±0.5 percent) from last year. Motor vehicle and parts dealers were up 6.4 percent (±1.8 percent) from last year, while food service and drinking places were up 5.4 percent (±1.9 percent) from January 2024.

The chart below is a log-scale snapshot of retail sales since the early 1990s. The three exponential regressions through the data help us to evaluate the long-term trend of this key economic indicator.

  1. The light purple line is a linear regression through the complete data series.
  2. The green line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2007 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the Financial Crisis.
  3. The blue line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2019 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the COVID-19 pandemic.

Monthly retail sales have been above the light purple and blue line since March 2021, signaling increased consumer spending that was most likely pent-up as a result of the pandemic.

The year-over-year percent change provides another perspective on the historical trend. Current retail sales are up 4.2% compared to January 2024. Here is the headline series with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Core Retail Sales

Core sales (ex Autos) fell 0.4% in January, coming in lower than the expected 0.3% growth. This is the largest monthly drop since January 2024 and the first decline in the past five months.

Core retail sales are up 3.7% compared to January 2024. Here is the year-over-year chart of core retail sales with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Retail Sales: "Control" Purchases

The next two charts illustrate retail sales "control" purchases, which is an even more "core" view of retail sales. This series excludes motor vehicles & parts, gasoline, building materials as well as food services & drinking places. The popular financial press typically ignores this series, but it's a more consistent and reliable reading of the economy. Retail sales control purchases were down 0.8% in January, lower than the expected 0.3% growth. This is the largest monthly drop since March 2023 and the first decline in five months.

Similar to the retail sales snapshot chart earlier, the chart below is a log-scale snapshot of control purchases since the early 1990s and includes two of the exponential regressions previously mentioned.

Here is the same series year-over-year. Current control purchases are up 3.8% compared to January 2024.

For a better sense of the reduced volatility of the "control" series, here is a YoY overlay with the headline retail sales. Note that the two series follow each other closely, but headline sales have more extreme highs and lows than the control series.

Retail sales will impact interest in the SPDR S&P Retail ETF (XRT), VanEck Retail ETF (RTH), Amplify Online Retail ETF (IBUY), and ProShares Online Retail ETF (ONLN).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SMH: Buy For The Long Haul
data.description:
The Artificial Intelligence (AI) revolution has without question been the leading trend, topic of discussion, and news breaker over the last few years. The developments made have been nothing short of astonishing for consumers and investors worldwide. Semiconductors are the foundation of AI. They provide the hardware that powers the complex computations required for AI to function and improve.

VanEck Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SMH) is an exchange-traded fund that invests in semiconductors and information technology. The highly concentrated fund seeks to track the performance of the MVIS US Listed Semiconductor 25 Index. SMH has an expense ratio of .35% and offers a small 0.43% dividend yield.

SMH has seen gains of 785% over the last decade, as one of the best performing funds on the market. Right now, trading at around $251 per share, SMH is off of its all-time high of $281 per share in July 2024. Pretty flat over the last 6 months, it feels like a breaking point is approaching for the ETF to start moving in one direction or the other. I rate SMH a long-term buy. The high valuation for the heavily weighted stocks is a short-term concern. SMH has crushed its peers, and I anticipate the dominance to continue in the long game.

Holdings

SMH carries just 26 stocks and is completely allocated to the technology sector. Almost 40% of the ETF weight comes on the shoulders of just three companies, Nvidia (NVDA), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), and Broadcom (AVGO). The fund is primarily United States based outside of TSM from the Taiwan region. With all large caps, SMH has over $23 billion in AUM, the highest among its semiconductor peers.

The Big Three

NVDA, AVGO, and TSM are the top three stocks in the semiconductor industry in market cap, and it’s not even close. As some of, if not the strongest companies in the industry, the heavy allocation to them has been a good matter for SMH.

NVDA and AVGO are competitors and far and away the market leaders for semiconductors in the United States. TSM plays a different role, which for several reasons I find a positive for SMH. The Taiwanese company is the world’s largest “pure play” chip factory, meaning that they only produce chips for customers under contract. TSM manufactures, tests, and sells semiconductors to both NVDA and AVGO. Almost 90% of the world’s most advanced chips, including the main processors inside mobile phones, AI graphics processing units, and personal computers, are made in Taiwan by TSM.

Looking at the quant grades for these three is extremely impressive. Gross profit margin for NVDA and AVGO is about 75%, with TSM at 56%. Revenue growth for NVDA has been out of this world recently and was 152% YoY, whereas both the others were still north of 33%. The elephant in the room is the valuation. All three have elevated P/E ratios, which I certainly see as a risk right now for SMH. On the bright side, forward-looking P/E is lower for all three and PEG ratios are at least respectable. Earnings have jumped quite a bit for each company in the past few years, and maintaining that speed of growth may be challenging over time.

Semiconductor Outlook

The semiconductor industry had expectations of $611 billion in 2024 and is expected to exceed that with $627 billion. $1 trillion in chip sales is expected by 2030, and with a modest 7.5% CAGR, the industry would hit $2 trillion by 2040. Even with the likely overvaluation right now, the semiconductor industry is a safe place to invest for the long term. Demand is only increasing as semiconductors are a building block toward the ultimate goal: AI growth.

Peers

SMH has significantly outperformed all its industry peers over the recent and long term. Over five years, the next closest competitor is still 80% off the mark from the returns of SMH. The difference is that all the other ETFs have a more balanced portfolio and have not reaped the same benefits of the excellent returns from the big three, specifically NVDA with 1,900% gains over 5 years. Of course, this lack in diversification can be painful in the dog days, but over the long run as an investor, I’d prefer heavier weight in the companies at the forefront of the industry. The liquidity is also a nice touch, as SMH smashes its peers in AUM and average daily share volume.

Risk & Macro Analysis

One of the more recently realized risks, at least to the general public, is the emergence of DeepSeek, a Chinese AI company. In late January, the company announced it had developed AI models for noticeably cheaper than anyone else in the world. As a potential threat, it took the markets to storm, with NVDA dropping 17% the day following. It’s still way too early to determine the effect DeepSeek could have on American semiconductor companies. But in my opinion, the grasp that NVDA and AVGO have on the market, with their history of producing chips along with TSM, is going to be awfully challenging for anyone to break into.

On the macroeconomic side, Trump announced, just days after his inauguration, the creation of The Stargate Project. This project will effectively spend $500 billion over the next four years building the AI infrastructure in the United States. On the other hand, the continued talk on tariffs is something for the industry to watch. Trump is looking to incentivize domestic production of advanced chips. That could create issues given recent lack of capacity, but luckily the Chips Act has created funding for the world's advanced chip leader, TSM, to build plants in Arizona that will hopefully be operable by 2030.

Conclusion

With valuation and the development of DeepSeek, there are some risks to consider with SMH. Ultimately, semiconductors are vital to so many industries today and are the backbone to the continued evolution of AI. The ETF will rely on NVDA, AVGO, and TSM, and still provide 60% weight to the remainder of the chipmakers in play. The growth and financial strength of those three are going to be hard for others to surpass. Investing in semiconductors isn’t going to be for a passive return. SMH provides the largest share of the industry leaders and is the right long term choice for chipmaking ETFs.

This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

data.title: Adobe: Undue Fear Makes It A Strong Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I have covered Adobe Inc (NASDAQ:ADBE) a few times in the past and have maintained a consistently bullish tone. When OpenAI's video generator Sora was released (causing a selloff in Adobe), I wrote that investors should ignore the FUD. I rated the company a Hold at the time based on a pretty rich valuation but felt that the market was overestimating the impacts of Sora on Adobe. Next, I upgraded Adobe based on what I called Very Compelling AI Monetization In Q2. The company has increased prices for products that include its Firefly AI model. Meanwhile, Adobe GenStudio is penetrating the enterprise market as a full-stack Martech platform. This will move Adobe further up the value chain and result in more pricing power over time. That's why the recent Deutsche Bank downgrade was puzzling to me.

When new technologies begin to proliferate consumer markets, investors immediately begin debating second and third order effects to predict the path certain stocks may take. The unquestionable winners of the first wave are the chip designers, foundries, server OEMs, and other names within the hardware ecosystem. The market is also focused on the expected winners of the second order effect of commercial AI: software.

AI will greatly improve the capabilities of software and be a disruptive force. You can't argue against that. However, we can't know with any sort of certainty what impacts we'll see in the medium-to-long term. What we can say for certain is that the market won't get it entirely right. There will be overhyped losers that don't pan out and long-term winners that get wrongfully punished in the short run. I believe this is the case with Adobe, as it has been getting punished on the prospect of AI greatly reducing the need for its software. Analysts are also concerned with monetization efforts.

As a result, Adobe has underperformed quite significantly this year. While management is very enthusiastic about the pace of product innovation, analysts are focused on the slowing pace of revenue growth. Despite this, Adobe's strategy is sensible. Tomorrow's winners will be dictated by who proliferates the market most effectively today and builds an entrenched user base. Adobe is a very clear leader in this sense, but the market is punishing the stock because these efforts have not yet translated to an acceleration in sales growth. The company now trades at a very low forward multiple relative to historical averages while the company has been cannibalizing its shares in recent years.

The last time the company's forward multiple was this low, it quickly mean-corrected in the preceding months. Since then, the company has also reduced shares outstanding by over 5%. This weakness creates an exceptional opportunity to accumulate stock in an incredible company.

Adobe's AI Opportunity

Many armchair prognosticators have said that "AI won't replace your job, someone using AI will". This is right and wrong. AI will definitely displace some jobs. It will also create new jobs and turbocharge productivity in others. The sentiment of this quote holds true for enterprises too, which is the reason I remain bullish on Adobe. AI won't replace software companies, rather software companies that most effectively integrate AI into their stack will eat the lunch of those that don't. This is the case for Adobe.

Adobe offers the most extensive set of software products for digital creators. Countless websites are built on AEM, Adobe Experience Manager, which dictates the style, placement, and look-and-feel of web components. Photoshop has become synonymous with photo editing. Students learn to be digital creators in Adobe products and experts rely on it for their daily needs. Movie producers rely on Adobe's video editing software. Marketers depend on its full stack marketing software that offers targeting, analytics, and content deployment services. Any consumer or enterprise that needs high quality creative software is dependent on Adobe for a full suite of creative software. No company has the suite of products to match this, so a company that wants to avoid Adobe will have a piecemeal set of software products to achieve their needs. This adds undue complexity and erodes efficiency. It just makes sense to build on Adobe, and AI won't be changing this. The Adobe Flywheel is as strong as ever.

Despite the company's strong current moat, it can't afford to sit still as AI proliferates. And it hasn't. The company has weaved Firefly, a family of Generative AI models, into its entire stack. Firefly surpassed 16 billion token generations in the most recent quarter, with innovative features like Generative Fill gaining traction with users. There's a slew of other features like text-to-image, text-to-video, scene-to-image, and audio translation.

AI is set to power the next generation of digital creation and Adobe is focused on providing the underlying AI infrastructure for enterprise marketing with GenStudio. The rise of digital advertising has necessitated an intense focus on "Personal and Relevant" marketing. Marketers have better insights on their target audiences than ever before, but the key is to use these insights to deliver the right content to the right person at the right time. Delivering highly relevant, individualized content allows enterprises to enhance marketing efficacy. Adobe is critical to achieve this, which has resulted in continued double digit compounding. In Q4, the company achieved $3.3 billion in Digital Experience revenue, which grew 11% YoY Net new Creative Cloud ARR was $405 million.

The company has shipped AI products at an excellent pace in both the Digital Experience and Digital Media segments. Within Digital Media, which is mostly the Adobe Acrobat PDF reader, the launch of an AI assistant is revolutionizing how people interact with digital documents. It allows people to directly talk to legal contracts, marketing documents, digital applications, and the countless other use cases for PDFs. AI Assistant conversations doubled QoQ in Q4. Document Cloud revenue reached $843 million, growing 17% YoY and net new Document Cloud ARR reached a record of $173 million. The company ended the quarter with an ARR of $3.48 billion, growing 23% YoY. The demand for AI features within Adobe's ecosystem is clear.

Adobe has been a leader in commercializing AI, AI research, and monetizing AI. Firefly was one of the first family of models readily available for users at scale, the company has an extensive R&D program, and Firefly features have a very transparent and reasonable pricing structure. AI is going to dramatically increase efficiency for creative professionals, the effects of which will be most pronounced for people that use Adobe software. It isn't going to drive people away from Adobe, rather the increased efficiency could increase demand for Adobe's AI-powered software. Such is the Jevons Paradox.

In short, I think the market is dead wrong here. The recent weakness in the stock has brought the valuation to an extremely reasonable level relative to historical averages, based on way overblown fears.

AI Fears, The Risk of Demand Erosion

The core of investors' fear toward Adobe is that AI will severely erode demand for digital content editing services. Anyone that has used OpenAI's image generator DALL-E or video generator Sora understands this risk. These products greatly enhance the layman's ability to create digital media, but it doesn't eliminate Adobe products from the market outright.

Enterprises that have built their entire digital ecosystems atop Adobe's building blocks can't just unwind this. They are locked in for the foreseeable future. A company whose website is built on AEM components, or a company that uses Adobe's Martech stack to power their digital marketing operation, or a company that uses PDF's extensively every day won't just eliminate their Adobe contracts because OpenAI's DALL-E can easily create cool photos. That's just nonsensical.

Still, analysts on the Q4 call were dubious of Adobe's monetization efforts. Alex Zukin of Wolfe Research asked about how AI will contribute to Digital Media ARR growth. David Wadhwani, President of the Digital Media segment, said that AI enhancements are "expanding our user base, increasing the value of core subscriptions, and creating entirely new enterprise revenue streams." In Q4, monthly active users grew over 25% YoY, surpassing 650 million paid and free users. This was mostly driven by strong top of funnel momentum through Acrobat Web, with monthly active users up nearly 50% YoY. The adobe.com domain saw record traffic in Q4. Driving this traffic was demand for Adobe Express, a suite of free software products, which acts as a phenomenal top-of-funnel vehicle to get new users introduced to the Adobe ecosystem.

Meanwhile, Keith Weiss of Morgan Stanley asked the most pointed question to management (emphasis added):

Keith Weiss (Morgan Stanley)

"A lot of excitement from you guys on the conference call about the pace of innovation... but apologies to state the obvious, investors aren’t feeling that excitement. The stock is a major underperformer year-to-date, and it's down again after hours today.

Despite all this innovation—new monetization avenues, pricing going up—the growth is going in the wrong direction. Is there some leak in the bucket? Is there share loss? Is there something that we’re not seeing that is taking away that momentum?"

Shantanu Narayen (CEO, Adobe)

"Keith, I understand the question, and I think if we take a step back and look at our full-year execution, we guided at the beginning of the year, then again mid-year, and then again at Q3, and in each case, we beat those targets. So, from our perspective, we are executing very well.

I think the question specifically around Digital Media net new ARR and Creative Cloud growth—one nuance I’d highlight is that Creative actually performed better than what might be evident at first glance. Acrobat’s contribution to Creative Cloud ARR increased, which means if you strip that out, the non-Acrobat creative products saw higher growth than what’s reported.

As we move forward, GenStudio will be a significant unlock for the enterprise, because it brings everything together in a way no other competitor can. AI in Firefly Services, custom model training, campaign automation, and brand-consistent content generation are already seeing strong demand.

Now, as to why growth isn’t accelerating even faster—AI is a multi-phase opportunity. First, we prioritize proliferation—getting AI deeply embedded into workflows. Once users are dependent on AI for creative work, monetization kicks in through new tiers and premium offerings. We’re confident this transition will translate into accelerating financial growth."

Management isn't directly focused on monetizing AI yet. This is the right strategy. Proliferation will win the day because users will become more deeply entrenched into Adobe's ecosystem. Usage will grow and full-stack workflows will create a very strong lock-in effect and a compelling flywheel for the company. At that point, it can focus on monetizing these users and compounding sales for years to come.

Investor Takeaway

Adobe has a superb moat, illustrated by its world-class margin profile of 89% gross margin and a 25% net margin. While its current moat makes the Adobe castle impenetrable, the company is not resting on its laurels. Management has demonstrated its willingness to respond to major technological shifts throughout the AI revolution so far. Even though many pessimists believe AI will displace Adobe software, or are worried about the lack of growth acceleration, the company is powering full steam ahead to supercharge its products with AI tech.

This will contribute to continued consistency in sales growth over time, which flows through nicely to profit growth. The company has compounded sales in the double digits for 10 consecutive years and the sudden proliferation of more efficient methods of digital content creation will not stop this. Recent fears have led to a very low multiple relative to historical averages, around the multiple that the stock fell to in the post-COVID correction and the inflation-driven bear market. Both times, the stock swiftly rebounded as the company continued to execute with excellence at scale. This time is no different. Adobe is a Strong Buy.
